latency_per_decision.note: Wall-clock is ~parity for the in-memory classical engine (both sides are dominated by Python/pandas per-call overhead). The runtime/$ win scales with how expensive each replaced node is - it materializes when the source nodes are frontier-model or remote-service calls (the paper's regime), which the calls-avoided metric captures directly.

tokens_per_decision.note: null for the sklearn path; populated only behind an LLM adapter

headline: Compiling the dispatcher into weights replaces ~3.9 source-engine port calls per decision with one in-memory predict, shrinks the decision payload ~1.7x (387B -> 232B), and needs only a 22-field context vs the 16-node workflow; a full rule-change recompile takes ~31s. Wall-clock latency is ~parity for the in-memory engine - the runtime/$ win scales with how expensive each replaced node is. Compiled stays shadow-only.
